title: Do Google engineers actually vibe code?
summary: Alberta Tech examines whether Google engineers use intuitive or "vibing" code generation methods. The video compares OpenAI Codex versus Claude Code capabilities and discusses coding culture at Google across 2024-2026.
writeup: A former Google senior software engineer who spent 6.5 years building YouTube features examines how AI coding tools are and are not used inside large tech companies. The video draws a hard line between Karpathy's original "vibe coding" definition (no idea what language the program is in) and the newer term "agentic engineering" (AI tools used with oversight, planning, and code review). It traces Google's internal AI adoption from basic tab-autocomplete in 2024 through Sundar Pichai's contested "25% AI-generated code" claim, and identifies two structural blockers that kept Googlers from true vibe coding: bespoke internal frameworks the models were never trained on, and the company's "Tech Island" culture of building every tool in-house.
key_points: Andrej Karpathy's original definition of vibe coding requires the programmer to have no knowledge of what language or code is involved; using Claude Code or Gemini CLI on a familiar codebase is not vibe coding by that definition, even if colloquially called that.

Karpathy also coined the term 'agentic engineering' to describe the middle ground: real engineering done by orchestrating AI agents and reviewing their output, with oversight and planning rather than pure vibes.

Sundar Pichai's late-2024 claim that over 25% of Google code is AI-generated referred largely to smart autocomplete (accepting tab-completion of entire methods), not agent-generated pull requests, which misled outside observers about the true state of AI adoption.

Google's 'Tech Island' culture, where every tool is built internally and open-source or third-party software is avoided, meant Googlers could not use Cursor or other external AI coding tools, and had to wait months for internal equivalents after each major external release.

Because Google uses bespoke internal frameworks (not React Native, not standard open-source stacks), the large language models had no training data for those frameworks, which fundamentally limited how useful AI code generation could be for Google engineers in 2025.

Engineers inside Google publicly complained about being blocked from popular developer tools, with at least one threatening to quit if access was not granted, illustrating how the Tech Island constraint became a retention and productivity problem as external AI tooling accelerated.

chapters.title: Defining Vibe Coding vs Agentic Engineering
